sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 46, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the objects clearly:
- ID=3 (W, white, 4 pixels) at [20,12]-[2015] - This is the green block (white W)
- ID=4 (N, light green, 12 pixels) at [21,12]-[23,15] - This is the green block (light green N)
Wait, the green block should be N (light green). Let me re-check. The green block is ID=4 with color N (light green).
Actually looking at the color legend: N=light green. Let me re-read the legend: N=light green. So ID=4 (N=light green. But wait, the image shows a green block. Let me check - ID=3 is the green block.
The blue blocks are:
- ID=2 (b, blue, 4 pixels) at [17,41]-[18,42]
- ID=6 (b, blue, 4 pixels) at [25,45]-[26,46]
- ID=9 (b, blue, 4 pixels) at [29,17]-[30,18]
The gray blocks (c, charcoal) are:
- ID=1 (c, 12 pixels) at [16,40]-[19,43]
- ID=5 (c, 12 pixels) at [24,44]-[27,47]
- ID=7 (c, 12 pixels) at [28,16]-[31,19]
The central blue bar:
- ID=8 (b, blue, 28 pixels) at [28,28]-[3139] - large blue rectangle
- ID=10 (g, gray, 20 pixels) at [29,29]-[30,38] - gray rectangle inside the blue one
The green block (ID=4, N) is at [21,12]-[23,15]. The white block (ID=3, W) is at [2012]-[2015].
Wait, the green block is ID=4 (N=light green) at [21,12]-[23,15]. The white block (ID=3, W) at [20,12]-[20,15].
So the green block is at rows 21-23, cols 12-15. The gray block ID=7 is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19.
The green block needs to reach the gray block ID=7. Let me figure out the path.
Green is at [21-23, 12-15]. Target is ID=7 at [28-31, 16-19].
So I need to move RIGHT and DOWN. The green block needs to go from col 12-15 to col 16-19 and from row 21-23 to row 28.
Let me trace: green is at [21-23, 12-15]. I need to go RIGHT to get to col 16, then DOWN to row 28.
But wait, there's a blue block ID=9 at [29,17]-[30,18] inside ID=7. So the target is ID=7 which contains ID=9.
Let me think about the movement. The green block moves 4 pixels per step (its size is 4 pixels wide).
Current position: rows 21-23, cols 12-15.
Target: rows 28-31, cols 16-19.
I need to move RIGHT (7 rows down, 4 cols right).
But I've been moving around a lot. Let me just try to navigate there directly.
From [21-23, 12-15]: RIGHT would move to [21-23, 16-19]. Then DOWN would move to [25-27, 16-19]. Then DOWN again DOWN to [29-31, 16-19].
Wait, the block is 4 pixels tall (rows 21-23, so 3 rows). Let me re-check:
- RIGHT -> [21-23, 16-19]
 DOWN -> [24-26, 16-19]
 DOWN -> [27-29, 16-19]
 DOWN -> [30-32, 16-19]
Hmm, that would overlap with ID=7 at [28-31, 16-19] after 3 DOWNs.
Let me just try RIGHT once and then DOWN three times.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    print(f"ID={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
# Green block is ID=4 at [21-23, 12-15]. Target ID=7 at [28-31, 16-19]
# Need RIGHT then DOWN DOWN DOWN
action(['RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
